title: Going Up
passage:
When the draper's shop of Halbeck & Sons installed a hydraulic passenger lift in 1874, the trade press assumed the point was speed: four floors climbed without the stairs. The historian Agnes Roth, reading the shop's surviving day-books, found a stranger story. For its first two seasons the lift earned its keep less as transport than as spectacle. Halbeck's charged nothing to ride it and advertised it rather like a fairground attraction, and the ledgers show the upper-floor counters — long the hardest in the building to let — suddenly taking more on Saturday afternoons than the ground floor, when crowds came chiefly to be lifted. Roth is wary of drawing a rule from a single shop. The novelty, she notes, wore off within a few years, after which the lift settled into the dull, useful role everyone now remembers. Yet for a season or two, she argues, the machine sold not the floors above it but the ride itself.
— Tomas Wren

questions.prompt: What does the text suggest about the Halbeck lift's importance in its first two seasons?

options.text: It mattered chiefly because it let customers reach the shop's upper floors far faster than climbing the stairs would allow.
It permanently turned the shop's upper floors into the most profitable part of the whole building.
Its worth lay less in carrying shoppers between floors than in drawing crowds who came to ride it as a spectacle.
It set a standard that rival drapers across the town hurried to copy for their own premises.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), short text. STEM-LAW: the stem asks about the lift's importance in its first two seasons but does not entail whether that importance was as transport or as attraction. WHY-KEY (C): the passage states the lift 'earned its keep less as transport than as spectacle', that Halbeck's 'advertised it rather like a fairground attraction', and that crowds 'came chiefly to be lifted'; the closing line makes it explicit — 'the machine sold not the floors above it but the ride itself'. C infers that its early worth lay in drawing spectators rather than in moving shoppers, one inch beyond the stated contrast. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = too_literal / contradiction — restates the trade press's speed assumption, which the text introduces precisely to overturn ('found a stranger story'); tempting because it is the intuitive purpose of a lift and is voiced in the passage. B = quantifier_upgrade — 'permanently' and 'the most profitable part of the whole building' overstate a change the text limits to 'Saturday afternoons' for 'a season or two' before 'the novelty ... wore off'; tempting because the upper counters really did take more for a while. D = outside_knowledge — rival drapers copying the lift appear nowhere in the passage; tempting as a plausible next step in a commercial-history narrative.

generator_meta.topic: early passenger-lift history: an 1874 department-store lift that earned its keep first as a paid-for-free spectacle drawing crowds, not as vertical transport, until the novelty wore off

fictional_source.byline: Tomas Wren (invented essayist); research attributed to invented historian Agnes Roth

fictional_entities: Halbeck & Sons, an invented draper's shop, and its 1874 lift (invented)
Agnes Roth, historian (invented; all findings invented)
generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout ('draper', 'day-books', 'novelty'); no AmE-only spellings present.
generator_meta.engineered_traps: too_literal/contradiction at A (the overturned trade-press speed assumption), quantifier_upgrade at B ('permanently ... most profitable part of the whole building' vs the text's 'Saturday afternoons' for 'a season or two'), outside_knowledge at D (rivals copying, never stated); key C is the balanced inference and is not the sole hedged option — B is stated in absolute form and A as a confident single-cause claim, so 'pick the qualified answer' does not select the key.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved from the passage alone: C is the only option consistent with 'sold not the floors above it but the ride itself'; A restates the assumption the text overturns, B overstates permanence and scope, D adds an unstated claim. Exactly one defensible answer. All entities fictional; no famous-thesis anchoring (Otis and the 1853 safety brake are deliberately avoided).
